original_abstract: Utilizing renewable electricity to convert carbon dioxide into value-added chemicals offers a promising strategy for mitigating climate change. However, the electrochemical reduction of carbon dioxide to propylene—a vital industrial feedstock—is kinetically challenging, as it necessitates complex C–C coupling involving an 18-electron transfer. In this work, we demonstrate the electrosynthesis of propylene over copper nanocrystals, achieving a peak geometric current density of −5.5 mA cm⁻². These nanocrystals, derived from a CuCl precursor, predominantly expose Cu(100) and Cu(111) facets, which appear to facilitate the adsorption of critical *C₁ and *C₂ intermediates. Notably, the production rate of propylene decreases significantly when carbon monoxide is used as the sole reactant. Through isotope-labeling experiments involving mixtures of carbon dioxide and carbon monoxide, we suggest that the rate-determining step for propylene formation involves the coupling of adsorbed or molecular carbon dioxide (or carboxyl species) with *C₂ intermediates generated within the ethylene pathway.
humanized_abstract: The production of chemicals using renewable energy and carbon dioxide (CO2) is one of the key methods for mitigating climate change. However, the electrochemical reduction of CO2 into propylene, a valuable commodity chemical, is highly kinetically challenging because of the C-C bond coupling and 18-electron transfer involved. In this study, we show the first report of the electrosynthesis of propylene on copper nanocrystals (NCs) at a peak geometric current density of −5.5 mA cm−2. Our CuCl-derived copper NCs contain preferred (100) and (111) facets that greatly enhance the formation of key *C1 and *C2 species. A large decrease in propylene production is found when only carbon monoxide (CO) is used as reactant. Using the competitive isotope-labeling experiments between CO2 and CO, we infer that the rate-determining step of propylene synthesis is likely to be the coupling of either adsorbed or molecular CO2 (or carboxyl) species with *C2 species produced in the ethylene pathway.

Our CuCl-derived copper NCs contain preferred (100) and (111) facets that greatly enhance the formation of key *C1 and *C2 species. A large decrease in propylene production is found when only carbon monoxide (CO) is used as reactant. Using the competitive isotope-labeling experiments between CO2 and CO, we infer that the rate-determining step of propylene synthesis is likely to be the coupling of either adsorbed or molecular CO2 (or carboxyl) species with *C2 species produced in the ethylene pathway.
